title: Region Proposal Network (RPN)
concept: The Region Proposal Network (RPN) is a lightweight neural network that slides over the shared feature map to predict objectness scores and bounding box offsets.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main role of the RPN?

options.text: To predict region proposal coordinates and objectness scores on the GPU
To classify the specific categories of objects
To perform NMS on the raw image pixels

exercises.explain: The RPN predicts candidate regions and whether they contain an object (objectness).

cards.title: The Internal Scout
cards.body: If you are defending a castle, you don't wait for enemies to knock on the gate. You place a scout on the highest tower. The scout looks out and barks: 'Something is moving over there!' they don't know if it's a horse or a soldier — they just detect movement. That is the RPN.

cards.title: Region Proposal Network
cards.body: The Region Proposal Network (RPN) is a lightweight network that takes the shared feature map as input. It slides a small window over the map, and at each position, it evaluates a set of anchor boxes, predicting an objectness score (probability of containing an object vs background) and bounding box offsets.

cards.title: Object vs. Background
cards.body: The RPN is like a security guard looking at monitors. The guard doesn't need to identify if a visitor is a manager or a customer. They just shout: 'There is a person at door 3!', leaving the identification to the office staff (the classifier).

cards.title: Sliding Over Features
cards.body: The RPN slides a 3x3 convolutional filter over the feature map. At each cell, it evaluates 9 anchor boxes. For a 50x50 feature map, it checks 50 x 50 x 9 = 22,500 anchor combinations in a fraction of a millisecond.

cards.title: RPN does not classify objects
cards.body: It's a myth that the RPN outputs labels like 'cat' or 'car'. The RPN is binary: it only outputs two numbers representing 'object' or 'not object' (background). Specific classification is done later in the pipeline.

cards.title: RPN Sliding Window
cards.body: Visualizing the RPN heads at a grid cell on the feature map:

left.text:
Is there an object inside?
Outputs: [Object, Background]

right.text:
How should we adjust the anchor?
Outputs: [dx, dy, dw, dh]

exercises.prompt: What is the primary output of the objectness head in the RPN?
exercises.explain: The RPN is binary and only predicts whether an anchor contains any object ('objectness') or is background.

options.text: A probability score indicating if an anchor contains an object vs background
The color histogram of the pixels

exercises.prompt: True or False: The RPN evaluates multiple anchor boxes at each sliding window position of the feature map.
exercises.explain: True. The RPN checks multiple anchor shapes (aspect ratios/scales) at each cell to catch objects of different sizes.
Class-agnostic means the network does not distinguish between specific object categories at this stage.
exercises.sentence: The RPN is class-___; it only determines if an object exists, not its specific category.

exercises.prompt: Which prediction is output by the Region Proposal Network?
exercises.explain: The RPN outputs objectness scores and coordinate regression offsets for the draft boxes.

exercises.prompt: You are building a custom RPN. At a certain grid location, a tall anchor box has an objectness score of 0.98, and the regression head outputs dx=+2, dy=0, dw=-5, dh=+10. What does this mean?

options.text: The model is highly confident there is an object in that location, and recommends shifting the anchor box right and stretching it vertically.
The model has detected a pedestrian with 98% accuracy.
The anchor box should be classified as background.
The learning rate is too high.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The high objectness means an object is present, and the offsets tell the network how to adjust the anchor shape to wrap the object. Next: let's learn how RPN and classification share features!
